author: @kadmow
text:
movember - earlyish?? 2023, all good causes are worth while.

 - I agree, one needs to enjoy 3d printing - or creating in general, as not everything works out without a sleepless night or 2.

Getting some custom settings totally dialed results in great satisfaction...  (software and advanced sensors have advanced the curve for a minimal cost - compared with even 5-6 years ago - that seems like the stoneage now)

ppl, who insist on using 3d Printing for inappropriate geometries are still tedius to deal with - when plain flat and cylindrical solids, together with plastic welding or epoxy may make better components, many tools exist the magic of 3d is just one.

cheers.

author: @leostarling5746
text: You don't need any of these ratchets! Connect 'the stalk' directly to the generator. As that generates AC, it does not have to run all the time in the same direction! A linear generator connected to the spring/rubber band would be even simpler.

author: @Renew_You
text: Rob, we seem to be living in the future. When 3D printing was introduced it seemed to me as the tool for replication. Just another example of facsimile in that files can be transmitted then replicated. We now have the tools with specialized scanning abilities to scan existing physical parts and accurately reproduce them from originals. We can model/craft an original part, modify it and reproduce it.

author: @d1v1s1onby0
text: what is a game changer is that some companies now are offering a service where you can upload your stl and they will produce it in a metal of your choice. So you can rapid prototype in plastic and when your part fits - you can then get it made in say aluminium, so you get all the strength you need. pcbway is one company - you upload your file and they will quote you

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @lagunafishing
text: To reduce print time for none critical parts on the cheaper printers, I tend to increase the temp of the hot end slightly to achieve a better filament flow. Its surprising how much luck you can push for a result lol

author: @RobertA-hq3vz
text: I love 3D printing but it has limitations.  Alot of those 3D printed parts will develop micro fractures under repetitive strain and break after a while.  Unless you're using carbon fiber or something more substantial. When I need something that is subject to strain I make it real thick. Tiny struts don't survive long.   As for the Wind stalk, its cute, but you're ignoring the fact that it will produce a horrendous amount of noise as it ratchets. The neighbors aren't going to like that. I'm also wondering how it would compare to a simple fan blade. If a simple fan blade system produces more energy than the Wind Stalk, then all this is a waste of time.

author: @TerrorTubbie666
text:
Talking about the software:
Do 3Dprinters ever update their software???

replies.author: @zapaholic
replies.text: Most do but it can be like any other electronics in that the older the item gets, the fewer updates come along, ie "firmware". Slice software, the software that creates the actual commands that the printer uses can have many updates.

author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
text: apologies for mentioning (again) but I made a curious comment on the previou Qidi XMax3 video and linked to a 2 minute video I made solely for your concern, on a spectacle "patent" idea I had, and hope you will take a peep at the short video to see the intention of the spectacle's redesign. As spectacles should be "unbreakable, flexible spectacles..." witthe correct polymer this will be possible, especially if the 3D printer is pragmatic enough regarding possible materials it can use. In fact I wonder if anyone will ever bother but a businessman with a very large distribution network after our non-discosure discussion said he would stock it in his global franchise however me - I'm not capable of CAD nor have any investment capital, but think you may enjoy the idea and design it in tinkercad, esp. if you have a scanner to scan someone's lenses, for the inner rim of one of the components. I'm not giving way the exact notion in this comment, so I hope you will review the previous comment on the previous video and the very short video I made for you. I was exremely ill and not sure if I would get a heart attack or stroke so put the effort in and made a video considering I have no funds or infrastructure to do anything whatsoever. However it appear I will not be dying at this present stage and am glad for it, I'd gotten so rattled about living, but I'm much better now.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am glad to hear you are better - but I am afraid I never get involved in other folks projects - good luck with it and i wish you all the best

replies.author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering hi thank you. I'm not sure you will read this. But I had relinquished the idea. But basically seeing this printer it is merely a redesign of spectacles with three soft silicon components, two sidearms that rest on the ears, and then the third central bridge containing the lenses. but the polymer is soft enough so that the inner lip can be peeled open to set the lenses inside. and the joint of the spectacle's "arms" adjoin with som tye of protruding nib-joint, so there is no lose screws, it's just a flexible spectacle that holds its form and won't break if you step on it :) Yes I am much better. I had simpy had too much wine and had had sleeping tablets also, and I realised I had forgotten how many tablets I'd had. I'm gad that I did not die but certainly rue the pain I went through! :)
